---
title: "Getting Started with an Agent"
---

Coding agents are powerful tools, but they come with significant risks. To use
agents safely, it’s important to run them in an environment where the potential
harms are mitigated. Coder Workspaces are very helpful for this purpose because
they are disposable virtual machines. Any agent running in a workspace can only
interact with the files you upload to the VM. This provides more safety than
running the agent on your personal computer, for example. 


## The Coder Workspace Environment

::: {.callout-note}
Coder workspaces are a UCSB-specific resource, however you could substitute
any cloud-based service for running virtual machines.
:::

Follow the [setup instructions](../setup.qmd) if necessary, to create a [Coder
Workspace](https://coder.dreamlab.ucsb.edu). You should have a workspace with a
dashboard like the image below.

![Coder Workspace Dashboard](../images/coder_dashboard.png){fig-alt="Example
Coder Workspace dashboard with buttons to stop and restart the workspace, and
open VS Code"}

Start VS Code from the workspace dashboard. The web-based editor starts in an
empty directory (`~/workspace`). 

Explore the VS Code interface:

- Create a new file, called `README.md` 
- Open a terminal window

![VS Code Web, with Terminal window for running bash
commands](../images/vscode_terminal.png){fig-alt="VS Code Web interface with
terminal window show output from `ls` command."}

### Git Setup

We will need to configure git in order to create commits later on.

```bash
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "you@ucsb.edu"
```

## Our Coding Agent: OpenCode

There are many coding agents out there. We like OpenCode because it is one of
the few widely used coding agents that is model agnostic.

To start OpenCode, run `opencode` in the VS Code terminal.

![VS Code window with OpenCode running in a
terminal](../images/vscode_opencode.png){fig-alt="VS Code Web with OpenCode
running."}

By default, OpenCode on Coder Workspaces is configured to use open weights
models through the College IT Gateway.

OpenCode needs to know which AI model to talk to. We'll point it at the **DREAM
Lab AI Gateway**, which gives us access to a range of models through a single API
key.

**1. Connect to a model provider.** In the OpenCode prompt, type `/connect` and
press <kbd>Enter</kbd>. Start typing `dream` to filter the list, then select
**DREAM Lab AI Gateway** under *Providers*.

![Selecting the DREAM Lab AI Gateway
provider](../images/opencode_connect_provider.png){fig-alt="OpenCode model
selection dropdown with DREAM Lab AI Gateway highlighted"}

**2. Enter your API key.** Paste the API key you were given for the workshop and
press <kbd>Enter</kbd> to submit.

![Entering your API
key](../images/opencode_connect_apikey.png){fig-alt="OpenCode prompt entering an
API key"}

**3. Select a model.** Choose **Gemini 3.5 Flash** from the list of available
models. You can use (Ctrl+F) to "favorite" a model. We will use both Gemini 3.5
Flash and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview (Custom Tools) -- you might want to favorite
both of these to make switching models a little easier.

![Selecting the Gemini 3.5 Flash
model](../images/opencode_connect_model.png){fig-alt="OpenCode model selection
dropdown with Gemini 3.5 Flash highlighted"}

OpenCode is now configured and ready to use the DREAM Lab AI Gateway.

## Our First Prompt

To demonstrate the capabilities of a coding agent, let's ask OpenCode to inspect
that machine we're using.

> Tell me about the machine you're running on: OS, resources, etc.

OpenCode will probably ask you for permission to run a bash command to
inspect the machine. It's safe to say yes because this isn't your real machine!

The response should look something like this.

```md
I will run a few non-modifying commands to retrieve information 
about the operating system, CPU, memory, and disk resources.

bash tool: "uname -a && cat /etc/os-release && free -h && nproc && df -h /"

This machine is running:
- **OS**: Ubuntu 26.04 LTS (Kernel 7.0.0, x86_64)
- **CPU**: 4 Cores
- **RAM**: 15 GiB (12 GiB available)
- **Disk**: 14 GiB total, 8.1 GiB free (41% used)
```

## Agent Permissions

Most coding agents, including OpenCode, have a **permission system** used to
control what actions the agent is allowed to perform in its environment. In the
example above, OpenCode paused and asked before running a bash command. Even
though our agent is isolated in a throwaway virtual machine, it's worth
understanding how it decides when to ask for your approval.

The actions the agent can perform (e.g., reading files, editing them, running shell
commands, fetching URLs) are determined by permission levels:

- **`allow`** — the action runs automatically, no questions asked.
- **`ask`** — the agent pauses and waits for your approval.
- **`deny`** — the action is blocked entirely.

By default **OpenCode allows most actions**, but you can change this through
OpenCode's configuration file.

A few defaults worth knowing:

- Reading files outside the working directory defaults to `ask`.
- `.env` files are denied by default, so secrets aren't read accidentally.

## OpenCode Commands

You can type "`/`" followed by a command name to configure OpenCode or perform
various actions. You can find a complete list of commands in the [OpenCode
documentation](https://opencode.ai/docs/tui/#commands). 

The commands we will use in the workshop are listed here:

- `/connect` - Add a model provider. Allows you to select from available
  providers and add their API keys.
- `/exit` - Exit OpenCode. (You can also use `/q`)
- `/help` - Display help information 
- `/models` - List and switch between models
- `/new` - Start a new session (fresh context)


## Exercise: Download and Plot NOAA Tide Data

:::{.callout-note}
Complete the exercise individually but discuss results in small groups (2-5 people).
:::

Use `/new` to start a new session (if necessary) and `/models` to select the
`Gemini 3.1 Pro (Custom Tools)` model. Then cut and paste the following prompt
into OpenCode:

> Create a new folder `noaa-tides` and use it as the working directory; then
> download monthly mean high water for all NOAA stations in California over the
> last ten years. Download as CSV and save in `noaa-tides/data`.

Wait for the agent to complete the task (it may take a few minutes), then answer
the following questions:

- What files were downloaded to `noaa-tides/data`? How many and what format? If
  they are CSVs (as expected), what columns do they include?
- Does everyone in the group have the same set of files? What similarities and
  differences can you find?
- Look at the agent's session transcript: how did the agent complete the task?
  For example: did it generate scripts to download the data and, if so, what
  language did it use? How did it construct the URL for the download request.
  (You can always **ask the agent to explain the steps it took**). 
- In your group, discuss the different approaches the agent took.

Try an additional prompt and discuss the results:

> "plot the mean high water value for all stations using a different color for
> each station. Include labels on x/y axes and a legend with the station name.
> What is the name of the station with the highest average mean high water value
> over the last decade?"

- Download the plot image using the File Browser tool from your workspace
  dashboard.
- As a group, compare the generated plots. Did everyone in the group get the same
  answer for the station with the highest mean high water value?
- In light of your discussion, how would you improve these prompts?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
### Discussion Notes

These are note from a previous run of the exercise prompts.. 

**What files were downloaded?**

The agent downloaded 18 CSV files, one per California tide station, saved to
`noaa-tides/data` and named by station ID (e.g. `9410170_monthly_mean.csv`).
Each file is the NOAA `monthly_mean` product, so even though we only asked for
mean high water, every CSV contains *all* the monthly datum columns (`Year,
Month, Highest, MHHW, MHW, MSL, MTL, MLW, MLLW, DTL, GT, MN, DHQ, DLQ, HWI, LWI,
Lowest, Inferred`). Each file holds ~120 monthly rows (June 2016 – May 2026),
and some cells are blank where NOAA had no value.

**How did the agent complete the task?**

1. The agent probed the NOAA metadata API (`mdapi/.../stations.json?state=CA`)
   and the data API (`datagetter?...product=monthly_mean`) with small one-off
   commands to learn how the API behaves.
2. It generated a python script, `download.py`, that looped over stations,
   requested CSV, and slept between calls.
3. It encountered some errors and worked around them:
   - The `?state=CA` filter on the stations endpoint was **silently ignored** —
     the first run started downloading *every* US station (Hawaii, the East
     Coast, the Gulf) instead of just California. The agent caught this, then
     fetched the full station list and filtered **client-side** on
     `state == 'CA'`, which correctly yielded 18 stations.
   - The API began returning `403 Forbidden` after rapid-fire requests (rate
     limiting). The agent diagnosed it with `curl -i`, then added a browser-like
     `User-Agent` header and increased the delay to 1 second per request.
4. Re-ran cleanly, then read one CSV back to confirm the format before
   reporting done.

Here is how the download request is created:

```py
url = (
  f"https://api.tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov/api/prod/datagetter"
  f"?begin_date={begin_date}"
  f"&end_date={end_date}"
  f"&station={station_id}"
  f"&product=monthly_mean"
  f"&datum=MHW"
  f"&time_zone=lst"
  f"&units=metric"
  f"&application=DataAPI_Sample"
  f"&format=csv"
)
```

**Highest average MHW station**

The agent reported **North Spit** as station with the highest average MHW. 

![Plot from NOAA Tides
exercise](../images/exercise-noaa-tides.png){fig-alt="Plot of downloaded station
MHW values"}

The agent averaged the `MHW` column across each file and reported **North Spit**
(≈0.154 m). That arithmetic is correct *for the data it downloaded* — but it's
probably not the best answer to the question.

**Caveats**

The generated script downloads data using `datum=MHW`, so every `MHW` value is
measured relative to each station's own MHW datum rather than an absolute
reference (see the [NOAA API
documentation](https://api.tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov/api/prod/#datum)). That
makes the numbers tiny (≈0.05–0.15 m) because they aren't tide heights at all —
they're **anomalies**, i.e. how far each month sat above that station's
historical baseline. So "North Spit is highest" really means *its* high water
has risen the most relative to *its own* reference. Ask for the same thing
against a fixed physical datum (`datum=MLLW`, the actual height of mean high
water above the chart datum) and the answer changes.

:::
